questions.prompt: Which of these best describes what the Huntress agent does on an endpoint?

choices.label: A signature-based scanner that sits between every file open and decides whether to block.
choices.rationale: That's traditional AV. The agent isn't a scanner in the AV sense.

choices: A background service that collects bounded telemetry.

choices.label: A user-facing tray application that shows scan progress and asks permission before each action.
choices.rationale: The agent runs in the background with no tray icon or popups.

choices.label: A network appliance that watches traffic from a SPAN port.
choices.rationale: Wrong product class. The agent runs as a system process on the endpoint, not on the network.

questions.explanation: (process and persistence inventory, behavioural detections, health and version) and sends it to Huntress's back end. The agent is a system service that ships bounded telemetry, not a file-access scanner and not a user-facing app.

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson insist on separating installation from registration?

choices.label: Because they happen in different cloud regions.
choices.rationale: Not the framing. The split isn't regional.

choices: Because they're two distinct steps that fail separately.

choices.label: Because installation is the customer's job and registration is the MSP's job.
choices.rationale: Wrong split. Both are MSP-driven via the RMM and runbook.

choices.label: Because installation is free and registration is billed.
choices.rationale: Not a real commercial mechanic.

questions.explanation: An install can succeed cleanly while registration never completes, leaving a dormant agent the RMM reports as done. The dormant-installed-not-registered state is the failure mode the split exists to surface.

questions.prompt: What does the agent use to register against a customer organisation on its first phone-home?

choices.label: The endpoint's hostname.
choices.rationale: Hostname is metadata after registration; it doesn't drive enrolment.

choices: The organisation key bundled with the installer or referenced by the RMM job.

choices.label: The customer's super-admin's email address.
choices.rationale: Identity-side concept (closer to ITDR), not the agent enrolment mechanism.

choices.label: An auto-generated random ID created at installation.
choices.rationale: A random ID without an organisation reference wouldn't tell the back end which customer; the org key is the bound discriminator.

questions.explanation: The org key is what tells the back end which customer the agent belongs to.

questions.prompt: A customer's IT contact asks 'does the Huntress agent read my files?' What's the right answer?

choices.label: Yes, that's how it detects threats.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the data flows. The agent doesn't ship file contents.

choices: No. It collects process and persistence telemetry.

choices.label: It depends on the customer's tier.
choices.rationale: The traffic profile isn't tiered. Same bounded telemetry across the platform.

choices.label: Only when the SOC asks it to.
choices.rationale: The agent doesn't pull file contents on SOC request.

questions.explanation: Not file contents, keystrokes, or documents. Reference the MSP's privacy / data-handling runbook for the published detail. The traffic profile is bounded: process and persistence, behavioural detections, health and version.

questions.prompt: Which platform list matches what the agent supports?
choices: Windows, macOS, Linux on recent supported versions.

choices.label: Windows only.
choices.rationale: macOS and Linux are supported too.

choices.label: Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, Android.
choices.rationale: Mobile (iOS, Android) is explicitly out of scope.

choices.label: Any platform with a JVM.
choices.rationale: The agent isn't a Java application; this isn't a real deployment model.

questions.explanation: Three supported platforms with recent-version caveats. Embedded devices, network appliances, mobile are out of scope.

questions.prompt: A senior asks you to confirm Huntress is on `SRV-DC02` at Contoso. The customer's Agents view doesn't show it. What's the right interpretation of the gap?

choices.label: The agent failed to install. The install job was never run or it failed mid-way.
choices.rationale: Possible, but only one of several causes. Portal absence means registration is missing; install could have succeeded with no registration following.

choices: Either the install never ran, or it ran but registration didn't complete.

choices.label: The agent has been uninstalled.
choices.rationale: With no prior state showing SRV-DC02 in the portal, 'uninstalled' is a guess. 'Never registered' is at least as likely.

questions.explanation: The RMM log tells you which. Portal absence is a registration signal. The RMM log discriminates between 'install didn't run' and 'install ran but didn't register.'

questions.prompt: Continuing the SRV-DC02 scenario: the RMM log shows the install job ran cleanly three days ago with exit code zero. The portal still shows nothing. What's the next step?

choices.label: Re-push the install.
choices.rationale: Premature. The install ran. Re-pushing without diagnosing why registration didn't happen risks corrupting installed state.

choices: Look at the agent's state on the endpoint.

choices.label: Tell the senior the agent is missing and ask them what to do.
choices.rationale: Wrong reflex. This is the work the senior expects you to do.

questions.explanation: Then check whether the org key bundled with the installer matches the customer's expected key. The install-vs-registration split tells you where to look: local logs for phone-home, org key for wrong-customer enrolment.

questions.prompt: The Huntress agent and traditional AV can run on the same endpoint without conflict, doing different work: AV blocks at file access, the agent watches behaviour and persistence.
questions.explanation: Two products at different layers, designed to coexist.

questions.prompt: The RMM reports 'install successful' on 200 endpoints; the Huntress portal shows 180. What's the right reading?

choices.label: 20 endpoints failed to install; re-push to them.
choices.rationale: The RMM said install succeeded on all 200. Re-pushing repeats work that already worked.

choices.label: The portal is broken; open a Huntress support ticket.
choices.rationale: The portal is showing the truth; the agents aren't registered yet.

choices.label: 200 is the wrong number; the RMM is over-counting.
choices.rationale: Both counts are correct; they're measuring different stages.

questions.explanation: 20 are stuck at registration. That's the gap to work, not 'the install failed.' The install-vs-registration split. Install vs. registration are two stages, two counts.

questions.prompt: An agent shows as 'online' but with a version from twelve months ago. Which lesson holds the diagnostic?

choices.label: This lesson, registration is the only check that matters.
choices.rationale: Wrong scope. This lesson establishes the agent model; version compliance is its own surface.

choices: The agent is running and registered;

choices.label: The agent is offline; version drift is one of the four offline causes.
choices.rationale: Version drift isn't one of the four offline causes from lesson 18. The agent is still online.

choices.label: The agent is uninstalled; re-install required.
choices.rationale: Online with old version means installed and registered, not uninstalled.

questions.explanation: Version compliance is a different surface, covered in the agent-upgrades lesson (20). Online with a stale version is silent drift out of effective coverage; lesson 20 covers the upgrade diagnostic.
